oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Action to Improve Population Estimates and Nest-site Availability for Cape Parrots
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Waugh, David
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 50 Number 1 (January 2023).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Action to Improve Population Estimates And Nest-site Availability for Cape ParrotsBy David Waugh Loro Parque CorrespondentFor a Cape Parrot (Poicephalus robustus) in Limpopo province, South Africa, travel to a pecan orchard is a piece of cake, because often the orchards are adjacent to the forest where Cape Parrots are native. The parrot's robust beak can easily crack the hard outer shell of a pecan to access the delicious nut inside. This is a situation less conflictive than it seems, because the Cape Parrot is a threatened species endemic to South Africa, with the current total population size between 1,100 and 1,786 individuals, of which only about 5.5% to 9% are found in the genetically isolated northern part of its range in Limpopo. Cape Parrots have suffered a reduction in geo-Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 50 No. 1 (2023): Watchbird January 2023; 24-25
